# Cooling

## summary

**Cooling** systems manage the heat generated by electrical components—primarily the CPU and GPU—preventing thermal damage and maintaining performance. Modern systems use air cooling (heatsinks and fans) or liquid cooling loops; thermal design power (TDP) ratings guide the required cooling capacity.

## you gotta know

- Excess *heat* damages silicon, reducing performance through *thermal throttling* (the CPU reducing clock speed to lower power consumption) or causing permanent *failure* if the component exceeds 100–120 °C for sustained periods.
- *Air cooling* uses a *heatsink* (a metal block, usually aluminum or copper, with fins to increase surface area) mounted directly to the CPU or GPU die, with a *fan* blowing air across the fins to carry heat away.
- *Thermal interface material* (TIM, usually *thermal paste* or *thermal pads*) fills microscopic gaps between the CPU die and heatsink, improving contact; poor application reduces cooling efficiency dramatically.
- *Liquid cooling* (water cooling) circulates cool fluid through a *water block* attached to the hot component, then through a *radiator* where a fan cools the fluid; water's high heat capacity makes liquid cooling more efficient than air, especially for high-power CPUs or GPUs.
- *Thermal Design Power* (TDP) is a measure (in watts) of the maximum heat a component dissipates at rated conditions; a 250W CPU requires a heatsink rated for at least that load, with safety margin.
- *Fan speed* (measured in RPM, revolutions per minute) and *CFM* (cubic feet of air per minute) determine cooling capacity; larger fans spin slower and are quieter; smaller, high-speed fans are louder.
- *Case airflow* — how air circulates through the chassis — is critical; *intake* fans draw fresh air in, *exhaust* fans push hot air out; negative pressure (more exhaust than intake) can draw dust through crevices, while positive or balanced pressure is ideal.
- *Over-clocking* (running CPU or GPU above stock clock speeds) generates significantly more heat; cooling capacity must be increased proportionally, or the component will thermal throttle and lose the performance gain.

## connections

- [[central-processing-unit]] — generates 65–280 watts of heat depending on generation; a CPU heatsink is mandatory, sized to the TDP.
- [[graphics-card]] — discrete GPUs generate 50–450 watts; on-card cooling (heatsink + fan or liquid loop) is built-in.
- [[power-supply-unit]] — the PSU itself generates heat and requires internal cooling; a PSU placed in a hot case may throttle if intake air is warm.
- [[motherboard]] — voltage regulators and chipsets also generate heat; airflow across the motherboard helps dissipate their load and extends component life.
